content.text: Every gradual rollout needs a way to stop itself. Rollout pause and rollback trigger criteria are the pre-defined, measurable conditions that cause a system — automatically or with human approval — to either freeze the current rollout percentage or reverse course entirely. For agentic AI, where outputs are non-deterministic and harm can surface subtly, these triggers are the difference between catching a regression at 5% traffic and discovering it at 100%.
What It Is

A rollout pause trigger is a condition that halts progression at the current traffic percentage. The system stays partially rolled out while engineers investigate. A rollback trigger is a more severe condition that returns traffic allocation to 0% immediately, routing all users back to the previous stable version.

The two are distinct by intent. A pause says: something looks wrong, but we are not sure it is fatal — stop expanding and investigate. A rollback says: we have confirmed or strongly suspect harm to users at the current percentage — evacuate now and analyze from safety.

Trigger criteria are the concrete threshold-plus-time-window conditions that cause these actions to fire. A condition like "error rate above 2% for five consecutive minutes" is a trigger criterion. It replaces subjective judgment ("something feels off") with an objective, reproducible test that can run in automated pipelines and produce an audit trail.
Pause triggers: halt rollout at current %, open an investigation window, require explicit human decision to resume or escalate to rollback
Rollback triggers: immediately redirect 100% of traffic to the previous stable version, then page the on-call team
Automatic triggers: evaluated continuously by an alert or pipeline; fire without human input when threshold is met
Manual triggers: require a human to press a pause or rollback button in a dashboard, typically based on qualitative signals
Why It Matters

Traditional software regressions are usually binary: a function throws an exception or it does not. The metrics that detect them — HTTP 5xx rate, null pointer exceptions, database timeouts — are high-signal and fast to spike. Agentic AI introduces a much harder class of regression: outputs that are syntactically valid, complete, and returned with status 200, but that are subtly wrong, off-tone, or actively harmful.

A new model version might hallucinate less frequently overall but produce confidently wrong answers in a specific domain. A prompt change might cause the agent to decline requests that are actually in-policy, reducing task completion silently. A tool-calling update might cause the agent to misuse an API in ways that create real-world side effects — file deletions, incorrect data writes, incorrect financial recommendations — that only become visible through downstream complaints.

Without explicit trigger criteria, teams rely on intuition and ad-hoc monitoring. This means: regressions are caught later (higher blast radius), decision authority is unclear (who can pull the rollback lever?), post-mortems cannot determine why an action was or was not taken, and regulatory audits find no documented safety controls. With explicit trigger criteria, every actor in the rollout — the deployment pipeline, the on-call engineer, the product owner — knows exactly what conditions mean stop.
Benefits
Blast radius control: catching a regression at 5% means at most 5% of users were affected, not 100%
Automation speed: an automated trigger can fire and reroute traffic within seconds — far faster than any human paging chain
Audit trail: every trigger event is timestamped and tied to a specific metric value, providing evidence for post-mortems and compliance reviews
Decision clarity: because criteria are agreed upon before the rollout starts, there is no ambiguity or politics about whether to pause when a trigger fires
Safety culture signal: documenting harm-rate rollback thresholds communicates organizational commitment to responsible AI deployment
Trade-offs

Trigger criteria introduce their own failure modes. Set thresholds too sensitive and you get alert fatigue: every minor blip pauses the rollout, slowing delivery and causing engineers to ignore alerts. Set them too lax and the trigger fires too late, after meaningful harm has already occurred. The calibration challenge is harder for agentic AI than for conventional software because baseline metrics fluctuate with prompt quality, user distribution, and time-of-day patterns.
False positives: a legitimate traffic spike from a sale event might temporarily push latency above the pause threshold, triggering an unnecessary halt
Baseline drift: as usage patterns evolve, trigger thresholds calibrated at launch may become incorrect — a cron job should periodically review and update them
Small sample problem: at 1% traffic, even a severe regression might not accumulate enough events to cross a time-window threshold before the next stage check-in
Metric coverage gaps: you can only trigger on metrics you actually measure; harms that surface through qualitative user feedback require manual triggers or dedicated human reviewers
How It Works

A trigger criterion has three components: a metric, a threshold, and a time window with a minimum violation count. The general form is: "metric M exceeds threshold T for at least D consecutive data points within window W." The consecutive-violation requirement prevents a single anomalous data point from firing the trigger — a common source of false positives in AI systems where individual requests have high inherent variance.

For example: "The 95th-percentile latency of the new agent version exceeds 3,000 ms for three consecutive 1-minute Prometheus evaluation intervals." This fires only if latency is elevated for at least 3 minutes straight, filtering out single-request outliers. The alert evaluates on the canary cohort metrics only, not the overall system, so a stable-version problem does not create a false trigger.

When a trigger fires, the action is dispatched to the rollout controller — typically a feature flag service or deployment orchestrator. For a pause trigger, the controller freezes the current traffic weight and emits a PAUSED event. For a rollback trigger, it sets the weight to 0 and emits a ROLLED_BACK event. Both events are written to an audit log, and a notification is dispatched to the on-call engineer via PagerDuty, Slack, or email.

attrs.alt: Rollout state machine showing PENDING, ACTIVE, PAUSED, COMPLETE, and ROLLED BACK states with pause and rollback trigger transitions, plus a trigger classification matrix

content.text: Architecture
The rollout trigger architecture has four layers: metric collection, evaluation, action dispatch, and notification.

Metric collection: the AI agent emits structured logs and metrics for every request. Each log record carries a version tag ("canary" or "stable") so metrics can be split and compared. A time-series database — Prometheus, Datadog, or a cloud-native equivalent — scrapes or receives these metrics and stores them with the version label. Quality metrics from a separate LLM judge or user feedback pipeline are joined against the same version tag.

Evaluation: alerting rules query the time-series database on a fixed interval (typically 30 seconds to 2 minutes). Each rule encodes one trigger criterion: a PromQL expression, a threshold, and a duration. When a rule's condition is continuously true for the configured duration, the alert fires. The alert system routes pause triggers and rollback triggers to separate channels — rollback alerts go directly to the rollout controller, pause alerts go to both the controller and the on-call pager.

Action dispatch: the rollout controller is an API that wraps the feature flag service. When it receives a pause event, it calls the feature flag API to freeze the current rollout percentage. When it receives a rollback event, it calls the API to set the canary weight to zero and record the rollback reason. The controller maintains an event log of every state transition.

Notification: a separate notification service subscribes to the rollout controller's event stream and sends alerts to Slack, PagerDuty, or email. The notification includes: which trigger fired, the metric value that crossed the threshold, the current rollout percentage, and a link to the runbook for investigating that trigger type.
When to Use It
Every gradual rollout of an agentic AI system should have trigger criteria defined before the first traffic increment is released. The specific criteria differ based on the agent's risk profile.
High-risk agents (financial advice, medical information, legal guidance): use tight thresholds, automated rollback for any harm signal, and require human approval to resume after any pause
Medium-risk agents (customer support, internal tools): automated pause on metric degradation, human decision on rollback; wider thresholds acceptable
Low-risk agents (content generation, search summarization): focus on operational metrics (latency, error rate); quality triggers can be manual based on periodic human review
Model version upgrades: even when prompts are unchanged, a new underlying model can regress on specific subtasks; always include quality triggers when swapping model versions
How to Implement

Step 1: establish pre-rollout baselines. Before touching traffic, measure the stable system for at least one week across different times of day. Record p50, p95, p99 latency; error rate; task completion rate; quality score (from an LLM judge); and support ticket volume per hour. These become the reference points your trigger thresholds are expressed relative to.

Step 2: define trigger criteria in a rollout specification document. For each trigger, record: the metric name, the threshold (absolute or relative to baseline), the time window, the consecutive violation count required, the action (PAUSE or ROLLBACK), and whether it is automatic or requires human approval. This document is reviewed and approved before rollout begins, so there is no debate during an incident.

Step 3: implement Prometheus alerting rules. Each trigger criterion becomes one YAML block in the alert rule file. Use labels to mark the alert as a pause or rollback action, and include a severity label for PagerDuty routing.

# prometheus-rollout-alerts.yaml
groups:
  - name: agentic_ai_rollout
    interval: 30s
    rules:

      # PAUSE trigger: canary error rate elevated
      - alert: CanaryErrorRateHigh
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(agent_requests_total{version="canary", status="error"}[2m]))
            /
            sum(rate(agent_requests_total{version="canary"}[2m]))
          ) > 0.02
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          rollout_action: pause
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary error rate above 2% for 5 minutes"
          description: "Error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}. Pausing rollout."
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/rollout-error-rate"

      # PAUSE trigger: canary p95 latency exceeds 2x baseline
      - alert: CanaryLatencySpike
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            sum by (le) (
              rate(agent_request_duration_seconds_bucket{version="canary"}[5m])
            )
          ) > 2 * on() group_left()
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            sum by (le) (
              rate(agent_request_duration_seconds_bucket{version="stable"}[5m])
            )
          )
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          rollout_action: pause
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary p95 latency is more than 2x stable"
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/rollout-latency"

      # ROLLBACK trigger: harmful output rate crosses safety threshold
      - alert: CanaryHarmRateCritical
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(agent_outputs_total{version="canary", harm_label="yes"}[5m]))
            /
            sum(rate(agent_outputs_total{version="canary"}[5m]))
          ) > 0.001
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          rollout_action: rollback
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary harmful output rate above 0.1%"
          description: "Immediate rollback required. Harm rate: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/rollout-harm"

      # ROLLBACK trigger: model provider availability degraded
      - alert: ModelProviderUnavailable
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(llm_api_requests_total{status="success"}[2m]))
            /
            sum(rate(llm_api_requests_total[2m]))
          ) < 0.95
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          rollout_action: rollback
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM provider availability below 95%"
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/model-provider-outage"

Step 4: configure the feature flag system to respond to Alertmanager webhooks. Most feature flag services (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, Unleash) expose a REST API to update flag rules. The rollout controller is a small service that receives Alertmanager webhook payloads and calls the appropriate flag API.

# rollout_controller.py — minimal webhook handler
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import httpx
import os
import time
import logging

app = FastAPI()
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

LD_API_KEY = os.environ["LAUNCHDARKLY_API_KEY"]
LD_PROJECT = os.environ["LD_PROJECT_KEY"]
LD_ENV = os.environ["LD_ENVIRONMENT_KEY"]
FLAG_KEY = os.environ["ROLLOUT_FLAG_KEY"]
AUDIT_LOG_PATH = os.environ.get("AUDIT_LOG_PATH", "/var/log/rollout-events.jsonl")


class AlertmanagerAlert(BaseModel):
    labels: dict
    annotations: dict
    status: str  # "firing" or "resolved"


class AlertmanagerPayload(BaseModel):
    alerts: list[AlertmanagerAlert]


def write_audit_event(event: dict):
    import json
    event["timestamp"] = time.time()
    with open(AUDIT_LOG_PATH, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(event) + "\n")


async def set_rollout_weight(weight: int, reason: str):
    """Update LaunchDarkly canary rollout weight (0-100)."""
    url = f"https://app.launchdarkly.com/api/v2/flags/{LD_PROJECT}/{FLAG_KEY}"
    # Patch the rollout percentage for the canary variation
    patch = [
        {
            "op": "replace",
            "path": f"/environments/{LD_ENV}/rules/0/rolloutContextKind",
            "value": "user"
        },
        {
            "op": "replace",
            "path": f"/environments/{LD_ENV}/fallthrough/rollout/variations/1/weight",
            "value": weight * 1000  # LD uses millipercent (100000 = 100%)
        },
        {
            "op": "replace",
            "path": f"/environments/{LD_ENV}/fallthrough/rollout/variations/0/weight",
            "value": (100 - weight) * 1000
        }
    ]
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.patch(
            url,
            headers={
                "Authorization": LD_API_KEY,
                "Content-Type": "application/json; domain-model=launchdarkly.semanticpatch"
            },
            json={"instructions": patch}
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
    write_audit_event({"action": "set_weight", "weight": weight, "reason": reason})
    logger.info(f"Rollout weight set to {weight}% — reason: {reason}")


@app.post("/webhook/alertmanager")
async def handle_alertmanager_webhook(payload: AlertmanagerPayload):
    for alert in payload.alerts:
        if alert.status != "firing":
            continue
        action = alert.labels.get("rollout_action")
        alert_name = alert.labels.get("alertname", "unknown")
        if action == "rollback":
            logger.warning(f"ROLLBACK triggered by {alert_name}")
            await set_rollout_weight(0, reason=f"auto-rollback:{alert_name}")
            # Controller does not resume automatically — human must intervene
        elif action == "pause":
            # Read current weight and freeze it (no increase allowed)
            # In this minimal implementation we emit a log event;
            # a full implementation would call a dedicated pause API
            logger.warning(f"PAUSE triggered by {alert_name}")
            write_audit_event({"action": "pause", "trigger": alert_name})
    return {"status": "processed"}

Step 5: configure quality metric triggers. Operational metrics (latency, error rate) are straightforward to alert on because they come from the request path. Quality metrics require a separate pipeline: a sample of canary responses is passed to an LLM judge on a schedule, scores are written to the time-series database, and a Prometheus recording rule computes a rolling average that the alert rule can query.

# quality-recording-rules.yaml
groups:
  - name: quality_recording
    interval: 5m
    rules:
      # rolling 30-minute task completion rate for canary
      - record: agent:task_completion_rate:30m
        expr: |
          sum(rate(agent_task_completed_total{version="canary"}[30m]))
          /
          sum(rate(agent_task_attempted_total{version="canary"}[30m]))
        labels:
          version: canary

      # LLM judge quality score (pushed via pushgateway by evaluation cron)
      # agent_quality_score{version="canary"} is updated every 10 minutes

  - name: quality_alerts
    rules:
      - alert: CanaryTaskCompletionDrop
        expr: |
          agent:task_completion_rate:30m{version="canary"} < 0.80
          and
          # only fire if stable baseline is healthy (not a systemic issue)
          agent:task_completion_rate:30m{version="stable"} > 0.88
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          rollout_action: pause
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary task completion dropped below 80%"
          description: "Completion: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}. Baseline: healthy."

      - alert: CanaryQualityScoreDrop
        expr: |
          agent_quality_score{version="canary"} < 3.5
          and
          agent_quality_score{version="stable"} > 4.0
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          rollout_action: pause
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM judge quality score below acceptable threshold"
Examples
The following table documents a complete trigger catalog for a customer-facing support agent. Each row is a concrete trigger criterion that would appear in a rollout specification document.

Real-World Example

A fintech company is rolling out a new version of their loan application guidance agent. The agent helps users understand eligibility criteria, estimate monthly payments, and decide whether to apply. The new version uses a model upgrade that is expected to improve explanation quality. The rollout schedule is: 5% → 15% → 40% → 100%, with 48-hour soak at each stage.

During the 5% stage, the CanaryTaskCompletionDrop alert fires: the task completion rate for the canary cohort drops from 91% to 73% over a 40-minute window. The alert evaluates as pending (condition true but window not elapsed) for 10 minutes, then fires. The rollout controller receives the webhook and freezes the rollout at 5%.

The on-call engineer receives a PagerDuty alert at 2:14 AM. The notification includes: alert name (CanaryTaskCompletionDrop), current task completion rate (73%), stable baseline (91%), affected user count (approximately 340 users), and a link to the investigation runbook. The engineer opens the runbook, which directs them to: (1) review a sample of canary sessions where the task did not complete; (2) check whether the drop correlates with a specific task type or user segment; (3) look for model error codes in the session logs.

Within 20 minutes, the engineer finds that the new model version is refusing to answer questions that include specific regulatory terms (such as "APR" and "origination fee"), citing an overly broad content policy. The refusals appear as graceful degradations — the agent says "I can't help with that" — which do not register as errors but do register as task failures. The engineer identifies the root cause: the new model's safety fine-tuning is miscalibrated for financial terminology.

Because the harm rate trigger did not fire (no outputs were harmful, just unhelpfully refused), the on-call engineer has the option to either rollback or resolve with a prompt fix. They add a system prompt clarification that explicitly permits financial terminology and redeploy. After 20 minutes of canary traffic showing task completion back at 89%, they manually resume the rollout. The entire pause lasted 47 minutes. Without the trigger, the same regression would have been noticed by customer support the next morning after 100% exposure.
Common Mistakes

Defining triggers after the rollout starts: the right moment to agree on criteria is before the first traffic increment. During a live rollout, there is organizational pressure to proceed and disagreement about thresholds is hard to resolve.

Using only operational metrics: error rate and latency tell you the system is broken, not that the agent is giving wrong answers. Quality metrics and harm-rate metrics are harder to instrument but are where the real AI-specific risk lives.

Ignoring the small-sample problem: at 1% traffic with 10,000 daily requests, the canary sees about 100 requests per day. A regression affecting 10% of requests will produce about 10 failure events — which may not cross a percentage threshold in a 5-minute window. Set absolute-count fallback conditions or widen the evaluation window for early-stage traffic.

No runbooks: a trigger fires at 3 AM and the on-call engineer sees a cryptic alert name with no context. Without a runbook link, they spend 30 minutes figuring out what to look at instead of investigating.

Treating pause and rollback as equivalent: pausing when rollback is warranted (or vice versa) wastes time or causes unnecessary disruption. Map each trigger to the correct action in the specification document.

Not comparing against the stable baseline: a trigger that fires on absolute canary metrics will false-positive during overall traffic spikes that affect both versions equally. Always evaluate canary metrics relative to stable metrics, not in isolation.
Stale baselines: if the stable version has degraded over time, its baseline is lower than historical normal. Triggers calibrated against a stale baseline will be too lenient.
Best Practices

Use relative thresholds for most triggers: express thresholds as multiples of the stable baseline ("p95 latency > 2x stable") rather than absolute values. This automatically adjusts for traffic patterns and seasonal variation.

Use absolute thresholds for safety triggers: harm rate should trigger at an absolute rate (0.1%), not relative to stable, because even if stable is also producing harm, you still want to rollback the higher-risk canary.

Layer automatic and manual triggers: automate the objective, fast-moving signals (error rate, latency, harm rate); require human approval for the slow, qualitative ones (support ticket sentiment, business metric trends).

Write a runbook for every trigger: each runbook specifies exactly what to look at, how to tell whether the trigger is a true positive or false positive, who can approve resume, and what conditions must be met before the rollout continues.

Test your triggers before the rollout: use a synthetic canary with known-bad behavior in a staging environment to verify that each trigger fires correctly and that the rollout controller responds as expected.
Set a maximum pause duration: if a pause is not resolved within a set time (for example, 4 hours), automatically escalate to rollback. A paused rollout that is forgotten is not safer than a rollback.
Review and recalibrate after each rollout: record every trigger event and whether it was a true positive or false positive. Use this data to tighten or relax thresholds for the next release.
Performance and Scalability
Trigger evaluation runs on the metrics infrastructure, not the request path, so it does not add latency to agent responses. The main scalability concerns are metric cardinality and evaluation lag.

Metric cardinality: every label dimension added to a metric (version, region, user_segment, task_type) multiplies the number of time series stored. A trigger that evaluates per region and per task type can generate hundreds of alert rule instances. At scale, this strains the alerting evaluator. Keep trigger metrics to the dimensions you actually alert on; use high-cardinality labels only in logs, not in counter metrics.

Evaluation lag: Prometheus alerting evaluates rules at a fixed interval. A 60-second scrape interval plus a 30-second evaluation interval means triggers can fire up to 90 seconds after the threshold is first crossed. For safety triggers where speed matters, reduce the scrape interval to 15 seconds and set the evaluation interval to 15 seconds. Accept the higher storage cost as the price of faster detection.

Quality metric triggers present a different constraint: the LLM judge evaluates batches on a schedule (typically every 10 minutes), so quality triggers have an inherent lag equal to the judge evaluation interval. The practical response is to combine real-time operational triggers (which catch hard failures quickly) with slower quality triggers (which catch subtle regressions over a longer window). Do not rely on quality triggers alone for time-sensitive detection.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Several tools compose the trigger infrastructure for a production agentic AI rollout.
Prometheus + Alertmanager: standard open-source stack for metric-based triggers; alerting rules are version-controlled YAML; Alertmanager routes alerts to webhooks, PagerDuty, Slack
Datadog Monitors: managed alternative with built-in anomaly detection; can express "canary value significantly higher than stable" without hard-coded thresholds using the anomaly() function
LaunchDarkly: feature flag platform with a rollout API; supports automated rollout progression and can be controlled via webhook from Alertmanager
Flagsmith, Unleash, GrowthBook: open-source flag platforms with REST APIs that can be scripted for rollout control
Argo Rollouts: Kubernetes-native progressive delivery with built-in analysis runs and automatic rollback; integrates with Prometheus and Datadog for metric analysis
Flagger: another Kubernetes operator for canary deployments with weighted traffic shifting and automatic analysis; supports custom metric providers including those backed by LLM evaluators
LLM evaluation frameworks (Langfuse, Weights & Biases, Braintrust): provide the quality metric pipeline that feeds LLM judge scores into time-series storage for quality-based triggers
Comparison with Alternatives
The main alternatives to pre-defined trigger criteria are manual monitoring, time-based gates, and statistical significance gates.

Manual monitoring (humans watch dashboards): relies on someone being awake and attentive. Catches regressions slowly, especially overnight. Provides no audit trail for why a decision was made or delayed. Not appropriate for any rollout with real-time safety risks.

Time-based gates (advance every 48 hours regardless of metrics): easy to implement and predictable. Appropriate for low-risk rollouts with very high confidence in pre-release testing. Does not catch regressions that manifest only under real traffic conditions. Missing the core value proposition of gradual rollout.

Statistical significance gates (advance only when the canary is statistically equivalent to stable at p < 0.05): rigorous and principled. Used by Argo Rollouts' analysis runs and some internal ML deployment systems. Requires sufficient sample size before advancing, which can make rollouts very slow at low traffic percentages. Well-suited for metric-based criteria but difficult to apply to harm-rate or safety criteria where even a single confirmed harmful output warrants investigation.

Pre-defined threshold triggers occupy the practical middle ground: they are fast (no waiting for statistical significance), explicit (no subjective interpretation), automatable, and auditable. The cost is the need for careful initial calibration and periodic recalibration.
Security and Reliability
The trigger and rollback system is itself critical infrastructure. A compromised or failed rollout controller could allow a harmful canary to run unchecked. Several reliability considerations apply.

Fail-safe defaults: if the rollout controller loses connectivity to the feature flag service, it should emit an alert but not silently proceed. Some teams configure the feature flag service to default to the stable version if the controller has not confirmed the rollout percentage within a timeout window.

Access control on rollout operations: the ability to modify rollout percentages, disable triggers, or force-resume after a rollback should be limited to authorized roles. A developer with push access should not be able to unilaterally re-enable a rollout that an automated trigger paused. Use separate roles for "can deploy" and "can override safety controls."

Audit log integrity: the rollout event log — which records every trigger firing, every weight change, every resume — should be written to a tamper-evident store. In regulated industries, this log is a compliance artifact. Use append-only storage (an S3 bucket with object lock, or a write-once database) and sign log entries.

Testing trigger infrastructure: run fire drills quarterly. Deliberately inject a known-bad canary in a staging environment and verify that: the alert fires within the expected time window, the rollout controller receives the webhook, the flag weight is set to zero, the on-call engineer receives the notification, and the audit log records the full sequence. If any step fails in staging, it will fail in production when it matters.
Mental Model

Think of rollout trigger criteria as a pre-commitment device. Before the rollout begins, when everyone is calm and rational, you decide: these are the conditions under which we stop. You encode that decision into automated systems. Later, when the rollout is live and there is organizational pressure to proceed and the on-call engineer is looking at an ambiguous metric spike, the decision is already made. The trigger fires, the system pauses, and the question becomes not "should we stop?" but "what is causing this?"

The rollout state machine makes the semantics explicit. A rollout is not simply "on" or "off" — it is in one of several states, each with defined entry conditions, exit conditions, and permitted transitions. The PAUSED state exists specifically to create a space for investigation without either proceeding blindly or over-reacting with a full rollback. The distinction between PAUSE and ROLLBACK is a design choice about how much risk is acceptable during investigation.

The time window requirement in trigger criteria reflects another important insight: agentic AI systems have high per-request variance. Any single request can be an outlier. What matters is whether the population of requests is systematically worse, and that requires observing a population over time. The "for: 5m" clause in a Prometheus alert is not bureaucracy — it is the minimum evidence requirement before the system takes disruptive action.
Key Takeaways
PAUSE halts rollout at the current percentage for investigation; ROLLBACK returns to 0% immediately — use rollback for confirmed or high-confidence harm, pause for ambiguous signals
Every trigger criterion has three parts: metric, threshold, and time window with consecutive violation count — the window prevents single-request outliers from firing the trigger
Cover four trigger categories: metric-based (latency, error rate), quality-based (task completion, LLM judge score), safety-based (harm rate), and operational (token budget, provider availability)
Automate metric-based and safety triggers; require human approval for business and qualitative triggers — this gives speed where it matters and judgment where it is needed
Express most thresholds relative to the stable baseline to avoid false positives from overall traffic patterns; express safety thresholds in absolute terms
Every trigger must have a runbook: when it fires at 3 AM, the on-call engineer needs to know exactly what to look at and who can approve the next action
Record every trigger event in a tamper-evident audit log — trigger events are safety control records and may be required by compliance frameworks
Pre-commit to trigger criteria before the rollout begins — this removes ambiguity and organizational pressure from in-the-moment decisions about whether to stop
